app.title: DNA Markers — Learning App
footer.credit: DNA Markers teaching materials · by Ratri Boonruangrod · Department of Horticulture, Faculty of Agriculture at Kamphaeng Saen, Kasetsart University
home.hero.title: Learn DNA markers by doing
home.hero.subtitle: Pick a marker from the sidebar. Change a primer or enzyme and watch the gel pattern update instantly. End each topic with a short quiz.
home.pick: Pick a marker type to start
home.classify.title: All markers, classified — inheritance × technology
home.classify.intro: Columns = how much genotype info each marker reveals (dominant = band present/absent · codominant = each allele has its own band). Rows = what lab technology reads the result. Sequencing is a third technology category — it reads bases directly, neither hybridization nor PCR-detection.
rflp.title: RFLP — Restriction Fragment Length Polymorphism
rflp.subtitle: Restriction digest + Southern blot with a locus-specific probe.
rflp.principle.p1: RFLP relies on restriction endonucleases that cut DNA only at specific recognition sequences — for example, EcoRI cuts at GAATTC.
rflp.principle.p2: If a SNP between two alleles falls inside a recognition site, the cut may be created or abolished. The resulting fragments differ in number and size between alleles.
rflp.principle.p3: Workflow: (1) extract genomic DNA → (2) digest with enzyme → (3) gel electrophoresis (millions of fragments appear as a smear) → (4) Southern blot to transfer DNA to a membrane → (5) hybridize with a labeled probe specific to the locus → (6) only fragments carrying the locus appear as discrete bands.
rflp.principle.p4: RFLP is a codominant marker — heterozygotes show bands from both alleles simultaneously, so you can tell homozygotes and heterozygotes apart.
rflp.sim.help: Pick an enzyme and click Run. Two views appear: (1) Stained gel = every fragment cut from the DNA. (2) Southern blot = only fragments the probe hybridizes to. Notice that heterozygotes show two bands on the Southern.
rflp.dataset.title: Example dataset: gene X in 3 individuals (diploid, 1500 bp region)
rflp.dataset.note: Every sample has two invariant EcoRI sites (positions 97 and 1397) plus one variable site (position 697) controlled by a SNP. A locus-specific probe spans positions 400–600.
rflp.view.southern: Southern blot — only fragments bound by the probe
caps.title: CAPs — Cleaved Amplified Polymorphic Sequences
caps.subtitle: Amplify by PCR, then cut with a restriction enzyme — RFLP-style results on a regular stained gel.
caps.principle.p1: CAPs combines the specificity and codominance of RFLP with the speed and low DNA requirement of PCR — no Southern blot needed.
caps.principle.p2: Workflow: (1) design a primer pair flanking a SNP that lies inside a restriction site → (2) PCR-amplify a ~300–800 bp region → (3) digest the PCR product with the enzyme → (4) resolve fragments on a gel — directly visible, no probe needed.
caps.principle.p3: Because the amplicon contains only the target locus, the gel shows only that locus's bands directly. CAPs is codominant, just like RFLP.
caps.principle.p4: Limitation: the SNP must lie within a restriction-enzyme recognition site (only about 1 in 4 SNPs do). When it doesn't, use dCAPs or ARMS instead.
caps.sim.help: Choose an enzyme and click Run — see how each allele's amplicon is cut, and how diploid genotypes display a clean codominant pattern.
caps.dataset.title: Example dataset: SNP in gene Y (480 bp amplicon)
caps.dataset.note: Primer pair flanks a SNP at position 250 — Allele A carries GAATTC (cut by EcoRI), Allele a carries GAACTC (SNP abolishes the site).
dcaps.title: dCAPs — derived CAPs
dcaps.subtitle: Extension of CAPs for SNPs that don't lie in a natural restriction site.
dcaps.principle.p1: Most SNPs are not in a natural restriction site. dCAPs engineers a restriction site by designing a primer with 1–2 mismatches near the 3' end.
dcaps.principle.p2: How it works: the mismatched bases on the primer become incorporated into the amplicon during PCR. Combined with the SNP base of one allele, they complete a restriction-enzyme recognition site; combined with the other allele, the site is not formed.
dcaps.principle.p3: Example: SNP is [C/T] with no natural site. Design the forward primer's 3' end as ...CCTGCA instead of the native ...CCTGTA. With allele [G] the amplicon reads ...CCTGCAG = PstI site (CTGCAG); with allele [A] it reads ...CCTGCAA — not a site.
dcaps.principle.p4: Result is codominant like CAPs, but the cut fragments are usually asymmetric in size because the engineered cut site sits near one end of the amplicon (at the primer position).
dcaps.dataset.title: Example dataset: [G/A] SNP in gene Z, dCAPs designed with PstI
dcaps.dataset.note: 220 bp amplicon — the engineered forward primer creates a PstI site ~22 bp from the 5' end only when allele G is present.
dcaps.wt.title: Sequence-level walkthrough — the dCAPs trick step by step
dcaps.wt.step1.title: Native template — two alleles differing at a single SNP
dcaps.wt.step1.note: G/A SNP at position 23 · no natural restriction site touches this position → plain CAPs won't work, so we use the dCAPs trick.
dcaps.wt.step2.title: Design a dCAPs primer — introduce 1 mismatch near the 3' end
dcaps.wt.step2.note: Goal: have the amplicon read CTGCA just upstream of the SNP — combined with SNP G this becomes CTGCAG = a PstI site.
dcaps.wt.step3.title: After PCR — primer puts a C at position 21 of the amplicon
dcaps.wt.step4.title: After PstI digestion — bands differ on the gel
tetra-arms.title: Tetra-ARMS PCR
tetra-arms.subtitle: Four primers in one tube to discriminate SNP alleles — genotype read directly from bands on an agarose gel.
tetra-arms.principle.p1: ARMS stands for Amplification Refractory Mutation System (Newton et al., 1989) — a PCR technique for detecting sequence differences (SNPs, small InDels). It relies on the fact that Taq DNA polymerase extends a primer efficiently only when the primer's 3' base is correctly paired with the template.
tetra-arms.principle.p2: A common student misconception: that a primer with a wrong 3' base can't bind the template. In fact the primer still binds normally — what fails is the DNA extension step of Taq:Matched 3' base → Taq extends → strong bandMismatched 3' base → Taq extension is 10³–10⁶ times less efficient → band disappears (or is very faint)
tetra-arms.principle.p3: 3'-end discrimination alone isn't always sharp enough, so most ARMS primers add a second mismatch just upstream of the 3' end (position −2; Ye & Day, 2001) to further weaken the duplex. With the annealing temperature tuned right:At the target allele: primer binds tightly, 3' base pairs cleanly with template → Taq extends → amplicon producedAt the wrong allele: the 3' mismatch + the −2 mismatch make the 3' end "float" off the template → Taq cannot extend → no amplicon → the primer is therefore "refractory" to non-target alleles, giving a sharp allele call
tetra-arms.principle.p4: Tetra-primer ARMS (Ye et al., 2001) packs four primers in one tube: an outer pair (OF + OR) produces a control band; an inner pair is allele-specific (with the −2 secondary mismatch + 3' end matching its target allele), amplifying in opposite directions → two distinguishable product sizes. Read directly from an agarose gel · codominant · ideal for developing functional / diagnostic markers, especially in breeding programs that screen many samples.
tetra-arms.dataset.title: Example dataset: [A/T] SNP in gene W
tetra-arms.dataset.note: Outer pair → 412 bp control band (always); Inner-A + OR → 248 bp (only if allele A); OF + Inner-T → 218 bp (only if allele T). The combination of visible bands gives the genotype.
tetra-arms.wt.title: Sequence-level walkthrough — ARMS principle + tetra-primer setup
tetra-arms.wt.step1.title: ARMS principle — matched 3' = strong band · mismatched 3' = band missing
tetra-arms.wt.step1.note: The primer still binds the template normally regardless of the 3' base — what fails is extension. Matched → Taq extends to completion → strong band. Mismatched → extension efficiency drops 10³–10⁶× → band disappears (or is very faint). Most ARMS primers add a secondary mismatch at position −2 from the 3' end (Ye & Day, 2001) to further destabilize the duplex and sharpen allele discrimination.
tetra-arms.wt.step2.title: Tetra-primer setup — four primers in one tube
tetra-arms.wt.step2.note: Outer pair (OF + OR) flanks the whole region → always amplifies a 412 bp control band. Inner allele-specific pair (IF-A + IR-T) have 3' ends at the SNP, each specific to a different allele. All four primers live in one tube → both alleles are scored together.
tetra-arms.wt.step3.title: After PCR — products depend on which template is present
tetra-arms.wt.step3.note: Every allele produces the 412 bp control band (OF + OR). An A-allele template additionally produces the 248 bp band (IF-A active). A T-allele template additionally produces the 218 bp band (IR-T active). Because the two inner primers sit on opposite sides of the SNP, their products have different sizes — easily resolved on a gel.
tetra-arms.wt.step4.title: Gel pattern — read the genotype directly
tetra-arms.wt.step4.note: Codominant: an AT heterozygote shows all three bands (control + both allele-specifics), distinct from either homozygote. One reaction, no enzyme cut, no special equipment.
scar.title: SCAR — Sequence-Characterized Amplified Region
scar.subtitle: A locus-specific marker developed from a sequenced RAPD/AFLP band — single PCR, results read directly from a gel.
scar.principle.p1: SCAR is the follow-up to RAPD, AFLP, or sequencing data. When an interesting band or genomic region is identified (e.g., a band linked to disease resistance, or a region found from whole-genome / RNA sequencing), its sequence is used to design a new highly specific primer pair.
scar.principle.p2: The result is a PCR reaction that is highly reproducible — no low-stringency annealing (as in RAPD), no adapter ligation (as in AFLP).
scar.principle.p3: Detected variation: usually InDel polymorphisms in or near the gene, producing amplicons of different size between alleles — visible as different-sized bands on a gel.
scar.principle.p4: For length polymorphism → codominant (heterozygotes show 2 bands). For presence/absence → dominant.
scar.sim.help: Example: length polymorphism in a disease-resistance gene. Allele R carries a 50-bp insertion in the middle of the gene → 480 bp amplicon. Allele s lacks the insertion → 430 bp amplicon.
scar.dataset.title: Example dataset: gene M (disease resistance)
scar.dataset.note: Allele R has a 50-bp insertion at positions 215–265 that allele s lacks → R amplicon = 480 bp, s amplicon = 430 bp.
ssr.title: SSR — Simple Sequence Repeats (microsatellite)
ssr.subtitle: Detects copy-number variation of short repeat motifs — codominant + multiallelic — the most popular marker in plant and animal breeding.
ssr.principle.p1: SSRs (also called microsatellites) are short repeat motifs (1–6 bp) repeated in tandem, such as (CA)n, (GATA)n, (AT)n, scattered throughout the genome (mostly in non-coding regions).
ssr.principle.p2: The number of repeats varies between individuals due to slippage during DNA replication, producing different amplicon sizes after PCR.
ssr.principle.p3: Workflow: (1) design a primer pair anchored in the flanking unique sequences around the repeat → (2) PCR → (3) resolve sizes on a high-resolution gel (4% agarose, acrylamide, or capillary electrophoresis).
ssr.principle.p4: Key advantages: codominant (heterozygotes show 2 bands) + multiallelic (often many alleles in a population) + reproducible + amenable to automation — the reasons SSRs dominate MAS, parentage testing, and population genetics.
ssr.sim.help: Three alleles with different (CA) repeat counts: 12, 16, 20. Amplicon length = primer (40 bp) + flanking (60 bp) + 2×n bp of repeat.
ssr.dataset.title: Example dataset: gene N microsatellite (CA)ₙ
ssr.dataset.note: Three population alleles: (CA)₁₂ → 124 bp · (CA)₁₆ → 132 bp · (CA)₂₀ → 140 bp. Ind-3 is the heterozygote 12/20 — note that you see two bands simultaneously.
ssr.cmp.title: Side-by-side comparison — two alleles with different repeat counts
ssr.cmp.note: Amplicon size differs purely because the (CA) repeat count differs — same primer pair, same flanking sequence. A difference of 8 repeats × 2 bp = 16 bp is easily resolved on a high-resolution gel.
rapd.title: RAPD — Random Amplified Polymorphic DNA
rapd.subtitle: PCR with a single short (~10 nt) random primer — cheap, no prior sequence required, but a dominant marker.
rapd.principle.p1: RAPD uses a single random primer ~10 nt long. PCR is run at low annealing temperature (~36 °C) so the primer binds many sites across the genome.
rapd.principle.p2: When the primer binds at two sites in opposing orientation within ~3 kb, that region is amplified — producing bands of different sizes on a gel.
rapd.principle.p3: Detected variation: SNPs or InDels at the primer-binding sites — if a binding site is altered, the primer can no longer bind and the corresponding band disappears.
rapd.principle.p4: Properties: dominant marker (you only score band-present vs band-absent) · no prior sequence needed · cheap and easy · downside: low reproducibility (very sensitive to PCR conditions) → largely replaced today by SSR and SNP markers.
rapd.sim.help: Simulation: one random primer · a 3000 bp region with 4 binding sites (S1–S4) · S1 and S4 are invariant · S2 and S3 vary between samples. Notice that Ind-1 and Ind-3 give the same band pattern despite different genotypes — RAPD can't distinguish heterozygotes from dominant homozygotes.
rapd.dataset.title: Example dataset: random primer OPA-01 (CAGGCCCTTC)
rapd.dataset.note: The S1·S2 pair amplifies a 600 bp band; the S3·S4 pair amplifies a 900 bp band. Ind-1 has all sites → both bands. Ind-2 lacks S2 on both chrs → loses the 600 bp band. Ind-3 is heterozygous at S2 (chr 1 has it, chr 2 doesn't) → chr 1 alone produces the 600 bp band → same pattern as Ind-1 despite a different genotype.
rapd.site.label: primer binding site
rapd.dominance.callout: This is RAPD's key limitation — dominance. You only score band-present vs band-absent. When a band is present you can't tell whether the sample is homozygous dominant (++) or heterozygous (+−). To distinguish them you need a codominant marker (SSR, RFLP, CAPs) or sequencing.
aflp.title: AFLP — Amplified Fragment Length Polymorphism
aflp.subtitle: Restriction digest + adapter ligation + selective PCR — hundreds of loci per reaction · a dominant marker.
aflp.principle.p1: AFLP combines the strengths of RFLP (restriction enzymes) with PCR (reproducible amplification) — usable on large genomes without prior sequence information.
aflp.principle.p2: Four main steps: (1) digest DNA with two enzymes — a rare cutter (e.g., EcoRI, 6 bp) + a frequent cutter (e.g., MseI, 4 bp); (2) ligate adapters to both fragment ends; (3) pre-amplification PCR using primers that bind the adapters; (4) selective amplification: primers carry +3 bases at the 3' end that select only the subset of fragments with matching sequence.
aflp.principle.p3: The +3 selective bases reduce fragment count by <sup>1</sup>⁄<sub>4³</sub> = 1/64 → ~50–100 bands per reaction (resolved on a high-resolution polyacrylamide gel).
aflp.principle.p4: Properties: dominant marker (band-present vs band-absent, like RAPD), but far more reproducible than RAPD thanks to stringent annealing. Surveys many loci in parallel — ideal for fingerprinting, biodiversity, and phylogeny.
aflp.sim.help: Three simulated samples · each lane shows ~15 bands per primer combination · most bands are monomorphic (shared by all samples); 2–3 are polymorphic (differ between samples) → enough to distinguish varieties.
aflp.dataset.title: Example dataset: EcoRI/MseI + selective primer +3
aflp.dataset.note: Sample 1 and Sample 2 differ at 2 polymorphic bands; Sample 3 has an additional unique band. Bands that appear/disappear reflect polymorphisms at restriction sites or selective bases.
aflp.int.title: Step through it — interactive walkthrough
aflp.int.help: Click a tab above to jump to a step, or use the "Next →" button below to advance one step at a time.
aflp.int.step1.title: Step 1 — Digest with two enzymes
aflp.int.step1.desc: Cut genomic DNA with a rare cutter (EcoRI, 6 bp — rare) + a frequent cutter (MseI, 4 bp — common). This produces three fragment types: M–M (MseI at both ends — most abundant), M–E (MseI + EcoRI — what AFLP scores), and E–E (EcoRI at both ends — rare).
aflp.int.step1.note: All three types appear in the bottom row, but in the end only M–E fragments produce readable bands — they need both an E-primer AND an M-primer flanking them for exponential amplification.
aflp.int.step2.title: Step 2 — Ligate adapters to every fragment end
aflp.int.step2.desc: Attach short known-sequence adapters to the sticky ends of every fragment using ligase. EcoRI adapter ligates to the EcoRI ends, MseI adapter to the MseI ends.
aflp.int.step2.note: Every fragment now carries known "name tags" at both ends — primers can bind here, letting PCR amplify the unknown DNA between them.
aflp.int.step3.title: Step 3 — Pre-amplification (+0 selective bases)
aflp.int.step3.desc: The reaction contains two primers — EcoRI primer + MseI primer (no selective bases yet). Exponential amplification needs both primer types, one on each end → only M–E fragments qualify. M–M and E–E lack one of the two primer-binding sites.
aflp.int.step3.note: Only M–E fragments amplify to ×10⁶ copies → become the template for the next step. M–M (abundant) and E–E (rare) are implicitly filtered out here.
aflp.int.step4.title: Step 4 — Selective amplification (+3 bases at the 3' end)
aflp.int.step4.desc: Switch to new primers carrying +3 extra bases at the 3' end. Taq can only extend when those 3 bases match the template exactly — fragments that don't match drop out.
aflp.int.step4.note: Result: only (1/4)³ = 1/64 of fragments amplify → ~50–100 bands per reaction — readable on a polyacrylamide gel. The pattern that survives is the AFLP fingerprint.
hrm.title: HRM — High Resolution Melting
hrm.subtitle: Detect SNP/InDel from amplicon melting-curve shape — single-reaction in real-time PCR, no enzyme cut, no sequencing.
hrm.principle.p1: HRM uses a saturating intercalating dye (e.g., LCGreen, EvaGreen) that binds double-stranded DNA and fluoresces. As DNA melts (denatures), the strands separate, the dye is released, and fluorescence drops.
hrm.principle.p2: After PCR, the instrument ramps up temperature in fine steps (~0.02–0.1 °C) and records fluorescence at every step → producing a melting curve.
hrm.principle.p3: Amplicons from different alleles have different GC content / sequence → different Tm (even from a single SNP) → distinct melting-curve shapes. Heterozygotes contain a mix of homoduplex + heteroduplex amplicons → broader curves with multiple inflection points.
hrm.principle.p4: Results are usually shown as a normalized difference plot (subtract a reference curve) → clearly separates homozygous AA, homozygous BB, and heterozygous AB. Fast, low cost per sample, non-destructive.
hrm.sim.help: Click a genotype below — the corresponding curve highlights. Notice that homozygotes have a single peak/dip at the Tm of their allele, while heterozygotes have two inflection points from the mixed homoduplex + heteroduplex populations.
hrm.dataset.title: Example dataset: 120 bp amplicon with a single [A/G] SNP
hrm.dataset.note: Tm(AA) = 81.5 °C · Tm(aa) = 82.8 °C (higher GC) · Aa heterozygote = mix of homoduplex + heteroduplex → broader curve with 2 inflection points.
hrm.range.caption: Top strip — Tm window where each genotype's amplicon melts. Homozygote bars are narrow (one Tm); the heterozygote bar is wider because of the low-Tm heteroduplex tail. Vertical guides project each homozygote Tm down onto the curves below.
hrm.duplex.title: Why the heterozygote curve is broader — strand re-annealing during the HRM ramp
hrm.snpclass.title: SNP class — expected Tm shift in HRM
hrm.snpclass.hardest: hardest — usually needs unlabelled probe HRM
hrm.snpclass.note: Our example uses an A/G SNP (Class 1) — well-separated curves. Class 3 (C/G) and especially Class 4 (A/T) often require an unlabelled probe to discriminate reliably.
kasp.title: KASP — Kompetitive Allele-Specific PCR
kasp.subtitle: Allele-specific PCR + FRET fluorescence — genotype is read directly from cluster plots. The leading marker for commercial MAS.
kasp.principle.p1: KASP uses three primers: a forward primer specific to allele A (with a FAM tail), a forward primer specific to allele a (with a HEX/VIC tail), and one common reverse primer.
kasp.principle.p2: A FRET cassette in the master mix carries a fluorophore + a quencher that suppresses fluorescence while they are paired. When an allele-specific primer extends the template, its newly synthesized tail captures the FRET cassette, separating the quencher and releasing fluorescence.
kasp.principle.p3: After PCR, the two fluorescence channels (FAM + HEX) are read and plotted on a scatter → producing three clusters: homozygous AA (high FAM), homozygous aa (high HEX), heterozygous Aa (medium of both) + NTC at the origin.
kasp.principle.p4: Strengths: extremely low cost per data point (~$0.10) · runs in 384-well plates · highly automatable · ideal for large-scale MAS in seed/breeding industries · today's dominant commercial marker.
kasp.sim.help: The scatter plot below shows a KASP result for 24 samples. Each dot is one sample. X-axis = FAM signal (allele A); Y-axis = HEX signal (allele a). Three distinct clusters let you read the genotype directly without running a gel.
kasp.dataset.title: Example dataset: [A/a] SNP · 24 samples + 2 NTC
kasp.dataset.note: Bottom-right cluster = AA (FAM-positive only). Top-left cluster = aa (HEX-positive only). Middle diagonal cluster = Aa heterozygotes (medium signal on both channels). Dots near origin = NTC (no DNA, no signal).
kasp.mech.title: Why each genotype lands in its own scatter cluster
kasp.mech.note: Top panel — how a single FRET cassette switches from quenched (no signal) to fluorescent: when an allele-specific primer extends, its newly-synthesised tail pairs with the cassette and displaces the quencher strand. Bottom panel — which cassette gets captured depends on the genotype: AA → only FAM, aa → only HEX, Aa → both. These outcomes produce the three clusters in the scatter plot below.
massarray.title: MassArray — MALDI-TOF SNP Genotyping
massarray.subtitle: Single-base extension followed by mass spectrometry — extremely accurate high-throughput SNP genotyping.
massarray.principle.p1: MassArray (Sequenom / Agena iPLEX) uses single-base extension (SBE): after PCR amplifying the SNP region, a primer whose 3' end abuts the SNP is added. Taq incorporates one base matching the template allele (using ddNTPs).
massarray.principle.p2: The four ddNTPs have different molecular masses — ddC (289 Da) · ddT (304 Da) · ddA (313 Da) · ddG (329 Da). So extension products of the two alleles differ by between 9 Da (ddA vs ddT) and 40 Da (ddG vs ddC), depending on which two bases the SNP swaps. MALDI-TOF resolves <1 Da easily, so even the smallest 9-Da gap is unambiguous.
massarray.principle.p3: Oligos are analyzed on a MALDI-TOF mass spectrometer → m/z ratios are measured → peaks appear at the masses of the extension products. 1 peak = homozygous (AA or aa); 2 peaks = heterozygous (Aa).
massarray.principle.p4: Strengths: very high accuracy (>99.5%) · multiplex many SNPs in one reaction · popular in clinical genotyping. Drawbacks: expensive MALDI-TOF instrument; throughput is moderate compared to NGS.
massarray.sim.help: The spectrum below is a per-sample MALDI-TOF trace. X-axis = m/z (Da); Y-axis = intensity. Blue peak = allele-A extension (m/z 5023); red peak = allele-a extension (m/z 5047). Heterozygous samples (Aa) show both peaks.
massarray.dataset.title: Example dataset: A/C SNP · extension products at 5023 and 5047 Da (24-Da gap = ddA vs ddC)
massarray.dataset.note: Peak at m/z = 5023 → allele A (ddA incorporated) · peak at m/z = 5047 → allele a (ddC incorporated) · the 24-Da spacing matches the ddA–ddC mass difference (313 − 289 = 24 Da) · peak height reflects extension-product abundance · Aa heterozygotes show two peaks of similar height.
massarray.sbe.title: How single-base extension turns one SNP into one (or two) mass peaks
massarray.sbe.note: Top panel — the four ddNTPs differ in mass by 9–40 Da · whichever ddNTP gets added becomes a chemical "stamp" on the extension product. Bottom panel — the extension primer's 3' end sits right before the SNP, so Taq adds exactly one ddNTP complementary to the template base · AA template → only ddA used → one mass · aa template → only ddC → one mass · Aa heterozygote → both → two masses → two peaks on the spectrum below.
marker.dcaps.short: Create a cut site via primer mismatch
marker.sequencing.short: Read the bases directly (Sanger · NGS · GBS)
sequencing.title: Sequencing-based markers
sequencing.subtitle: Read the actual base sequence — Sanger, next-generation sequencing (NGS), genotyping-by-sequencing (GBS), amplicon-seq.
sequencing.cs.heading: Coming soon
sequencing.cs.body1: This chapter is still being written. Sequencing-based genotyping deserves its own walk-through because it is the gold standard for both SNP and InDel detection, and the platform underlying every recent marker innovation (KASP design, GWAS, MAS pipelines, GBS).
sequencing.cs.topic1: Sanger sequencing — chain-termination chemistry · chromatogram reading · still the reference for individual loci.
sequencing.cs.topic2: Next-generation sequencing (NGS) — short-read platforms (Illumina), long-read platforms (PacBio / Nanopore) · cost trajectory.
sequencing.cs.topic3: Genotyping-by-sequencing (GBS) — restriction-site reduced representation + multiplexed barcodes → thousands of SNPs per sample at low cost.
sequencing.cs.topic4: Amplicon-seq / targeted panels — middle ground between KASP and whole-genome NGS.
sequencing.cs.cta: Until this chapter ships, see the discovery timeline in the foundation page for the historical context.
foundation.title: DNA marker pre-class foundation
foundation.toc.title: Contents
foundation.section1.heading: Why learn DNA markers?
foundation.section1.body:
A DNA marker is a laboratory technique that detects differences in DNA sequence between individuals. These differences — whether single-base changes (SNPs) or insertions/deletions (InDels) — let us read an individual's genotype without waiting for the phenotype to develop.

In plant and animal breeding this is the core of Marker-Assisted Selection (MAS): breeders can select seedlings carrying the target gene long before harvest, saving years of trial space and time.

This page builds up your knowledge in three steps:

1
Core concepts

Genome structure · locus · allele · SNP/InDel · consensus sequence

2
Match marker to variation

Which marker fits which type of variation?

3
Run the experiment

Open the marker app → simulate · digest · gel · quiz
foundation.section2.heading: Genome structure — where do markers live?
foundation.section2.intro:
To understand what each marker detects, we first need a map of the genome — because different markers target different regions.

Click any box to see which markers target that region.
foundation.section2.detail.title: Details
foundation.section2.detail.placeholder: Click a box in the diagram to see its description.
foundation.section3.heading: Locus & Allele on homologous chromosomes
foundation.section3.intro: Locus = the position of a gene on a chromosome. Allele = a variant form of the gene at that locus. Diploid organisms carry chromosomes in pairs (homologous chromosomes), so every locus has two alleles — either identical (homozygous) or different (heterozygous).
foundation.section3.tryItInstruction: Click any locus on the homologous chromosomes below, then change the genotype to see how the actual DNA sequence differs between AA, Aa, and aa.
foundation.section3.controls.title: Set genotype:
foundation.section3.note: In the marker app — RFLP, CAPs, dCAPs and Tetra-ARMS are codominant markers like this. That's why heterozygotes can be told apart from homozygotes from the band pattern alone.
foundation.section3.figureHeading: Three possible genotypes at one locus
foundation.section3.figureCaption: Each allele is a real double-stranded DNA helix on one of the two homologous chromosomes. At the same locus, allele A differs from allele a by a single SNP (highlighted in yellow). A heterozygous individual carries two helices with different base pairs at that position on the two homologous chromosomes.
foundation.section3.tryItHeading: Try it — click a locus and change the genotype
foundation.section4.heading: Variation: SNP vs InDel vs SSR
foundation.section4.intro: Three kinds of sequence variation are commonly exploited as DNA markers:
foundation.section4.snpDesc: A change at a single base position — like a one-letter typo (CAT → BAT).
foundation.section4.indelDesc: Insertion or deletion of one or more bases — like adding or removing letters in a word.
foundation.section4.ssrOverviewDesc: Differences in the number of repeats of a short motif (e.g. (CA)ₙ) inside a microsatellite. This is the variation used by SSR markers — codominant and multiallelic.
foundation.section4.frameshift: Frameshift mutation: if an InDel inside a coding region changes the base count by a number not divisible by 3, the reading frame shifts and the entire downstream protein is mistranslated. SSR copy variations rarely cause this problem because repeat tracts mostly sit outside coding regions.
foundation.section4.ssr.title: SSR copy number variation — different number of tandem repeats
foundation.section4.ssr.desc: A variation caused by gain/loss of short repeat units (e.g. (CA)n) inside a microsatellite. After PCR the amplicons differ in size — this is the basis of the SSR marker (codominant, multiallelic).
foundation.section4.ssr.note: Both alleles share identical flanking sequences. The central (CA) repeat differs — Allele 1 has 5 copies, Allele 2 has 8 copies → 6 bp size difference on the gel.
foundation.section5.heading: Reading chromatograms (Sanger trace)
foundation.section5.intro:
A Sanger sequencing result is displayed as a chromatogram — a plot showing the fluorescence signal of all four bases (A, C, G, T) at every position in the DNA sequence.

Reading the chromatogram of a diploid sample is a key way to detect heterozygous SNPs — you look for two overlapping peaks at a single position.
foundation.section5.sample1.title: Sample 1 — Homozygous (base G at the SNP position)
foundation.section5.sample2.title: Sample 2 — Heterozygous (bases G + A → IUPAC "R")
foundation.section5.legend.title: Chromatogram base colors
foundation.section5.insight: Key observation: at a heterozygous SNP position (arrow), you see two overlapping peaks at about half height. Sequencing software then calls the position with an IUPAC ambiguity code — R (A/G), Y (C/T), M (A/C), etc. This is how genotype is read directly from sequencing data.
foundation.section5.iupacTitle: IUPAC ambiguity codes (for heterozygous positions on chromatograms)
foundation.section6.heading: Which variation → which marker?
foundation.section6.intro: Different variations call for different markers. The table below maps variation type to suitable markers — click any marker name to open its simulation in the marker app.
foundation.section6.row.snp.principle: Restriction enzymes, or primers specific to the altered base
foundation.section6.row.indel.principle: PCR-based sizing — the amplicon length changes with the InDel
foundation.markerTypes.heading: Marker types — classified by inheritance × technology
foundation.markerTypes.intro: Every DNA marker can be classified along two independent axes: (1) inheritance — how much genotype information the marker reveals — and (2) detection technology — what lab technique is used to read it. These two axes together determine which marker is right for a given job.
foundation.markerTypes.axis1.title: Axis 1 · Inheritance — dominant vs codominant
foundation.markerTypes.dom.heading: Dominant marker — band present / absent
foundation.markerTypes.dom.body: Only one of the two alleles produces a band on the gel. The other allele is invisible ("null allele"). On the gel, individuals AA and Aa look identical — both show the band — while only aa is distinguishable (no band). You can detect the presence of the A allele but you cannot tell a homozygote from a heterozygote.
foundation.markerTypes.codom.heading: Codominant marker — both alleles visible
foundation.markerTypes.codom.body: Each allele produces its own distinguishable band (different size, melting curve, or fluorescence cluster). AA, Aa, and aa give three different patterns — the heterozygote shows both bands. This is the information-rich choice for any analysis that needs to count alleles.
foundation.markerTypes.axis2.title: Axis 2 · Detection technology — hybridization · PCR · sequencing
foundation.markerTypes.hybrid.heading: Hybridization-based — probe binds the target DNA
foundation.markerTypes.hybrid.body: Genomic DNA is cut with an enzyme, separated on a gel, transferred to a membrane, and detected with a labelled probe that hybridizes to its complementary sequence (Southern blot). No amplification step — the probe finds the target directly.
foundation.markerTypes.hybrid.pro: Robust, works without knowing the flanking sequence, very specific.
foundation.markerTypes.hybrid.con: Needs micrograms of DNA, several days of work, traditionally uses radioactivity. Largely replaced by PCR-based methods today.
foundation.markerTypes.pcr.heading: PCR-based — primers amplify the target region
foundation.markerTypes.pcr.body: Specific primers anneal to flanking sequences and Taq polymerase amplifies the locus a million-fold. Detection then uses gel size, restriction digest, fluorescence, melting curve, or mass — whatever the marker type calls for.
foundation.markerTypes.pcr.pro: Nanograms of DNA is enough, fast (hours not days), automatable, sensitive, no radioactivity.
foundation.markerTypes.pcr.con: Requires known flanking sequence to design primers. Can produce artefacts (non-specific bands, primer-dimers) if conditions aren't optimised.
foundation.markerTypes.matrix.title: Where each marker sits — 3 × 2 matrix
foundation.markerTypes.matrix.intro: Click any marker chip to jump straight into its simulation page. The third row (Sequencing-based) reads bases directly — a paradigm outside the classic hybridization vs PCR split.
foundation.markerTypes.tips.title: Practical tips for choosing a marker
foundation.markerTypes.tips.t1: Fingerprinting or DNA barcoding — a dominant marker (RAPD/AFLP) is enough and is cheaper.
foundation.markerTypes.tips.t2: MAS, parentage testing, population genetics — you need to count alleles, so pick a codominant marker (SSR, KASP, HRM…).
foundation.markerTypes.tips.t3: You know the target sequence in advance — design CAPs / dCAPs / Tetra-ARMS / HRM / KASP. They are fast and SNP-specific.
foundation.markerTypes.tips.t4: You don't know the sequence yet — start with a dominant scan (RAPD/AFLP) to find polymorphic bands, sequence them, then convert to a codominant SCAR/CAPs marker for routine use.
foundation.section7.heading: Timeline of discoveries
foundation.section7.intro: Every technique in this course rests on a chain of discoveries since the late 1800s — click any milestone to read more.
foundation.endCta.text: Ready? Try the markers in the virtual lab.
